summary: Julia Evans articulates her teaching philosophy: instead of explaining topics from the ground up, she targets specific knowledge gaps that experienced practitioners already have. She argues that most programmers learn 'backwards' — building things first with abstractions, then filling in foundational knowledge later. This mirrors how people actually work: you set up a website before learning HTTP, learn Rails before understanding SQL. Her approach assumes readers already know a lot, identifies the one or two things they're missing, and explains just those gaps. She traces this style to her experience as a drop-in math tutor, where she had 20 minutes to diagnose what someone didn't know and fill in the minimum useful information.
key_points: Most programmers learn 'backwards' — they build with abstractions first and fill in foundational knowledge later, and this is fine
Modern jobs have more abstractions than ever, so knowledge gaps about underlying systems are natural and expected
Effective teaching assumes the learner already knows a lot, identifies specific gaps, and explains just one concept at a time
Bugs are the best natural way to learn what's underneath an abstraction because they break through abstraction boundaries
Ranting about what 'kids these days' should know is boring and ineffective — providing targeted useful information works better
money_quotes: You can make a website and set up nginx or Apache by copying some stuff you found on Stack Overflow and didn't fully understand, and then you have a website on the internet!
I think that learning about HTTP headers after you already have a website is way more fun and a lot easier than learning about them before you have a website.
Abstractions are great, but they're also leaky, and to do great work you sometimes need to learn about what lives underneath the abstraction.
We've had that argument a billion times, and it's ineffective – people are going to keep learning abstractions first! It's fun to learn abstractions first!
One mistake I see people make a lot when they notice someone has a knowledge gap is assume 'wow, you don't know how binary works, you must not know ANYTHING.'

I learned to listen a lot and not jump to conclusions about what the person didn't know – often they already actually understood a lot about the math class they were taking and I just needed to explain one or two small things.
I think 'show people what lives underneath their abstractions' is a big part of what I'm trying to do with my writing.
key_insight: The most effective technical teaching doesn't start from the beginning — it meets people where they are, identifies the specific gap in their existing knowledge, and fills in just that one piece.
